controls.description: This control groups the first deterministic cluster-hardening checks around Pod Security posture: missing namespace enforcement labels, unsafe hostPath usage, and edge cases that undermine expected pod security restrictions.

live_signals: workloads running with host filesystem access
admission behavior diverging from expected pod-security posture

controls.name: Pod security and hostPath baseline

pattern_refs.name: hostPath PV bypasses Pod Security Policy restrictions
Pod Security Admission rejects lowercase capability "all"
Deployment mounts hostPath volume
Namespace lacks Pod Security Standards enforcement labels

guidance: Enforce Pod Security Standards labels at the namespace boundary by default.
Treat hostPath mounts as reviewed exceptions rather than normal workload configuration.
Validate pod security policy and admission behavior with real manifests, not only policy intent.

controls.summary: Detect namespace and workload defaults that weaken pod security boundaries or reintroduce host filesystem access.

controls.description: This control groups the certificate lifecycle and TLS configuration failures that operators need surfaced before they become node outages or cluster-wide trust failures: kubelet rotation problems, weak kubelet TLS posture, expired API server certificates, and broken reloading of rotated client certs.

live_signals: expired or stale certs
node or control-plane disruption after rotation

controls.name: Control plane certificate baseline

pattern_refs.name: Kubernetes API server loopback certificate expires after 1 year
Kubernetes kubelet certificate rotation failures cause service disruption
kubelet certificate rotation fails silently
Kubelet accepts weak TLS ciphersuites by default
Kubelet uses wrong certificate on startup after rotation
API server does not reload rotated kubelet client certificate
kubelet TLS bootstrap certificate not valid for node hostname
apiserver-etcd-client certificate expired breaks cluster

guidance: Monitor certificate rotation and expiry as part of cluster health, not only during upgrades.
Prefer explicit, modern TLS posture for kubelet and control-plane components.
Validate both issuance and reload behavior when rotating kubelet or API server certificates.

controls.summary: Detect certificate rotation and TLS posture failures that quietly degrade kubelet and control-plane trust.

controls.description: This control packages the admission-webhook reliability failures that have cluster-wide blast radius: webhooks unreachable from the API server, timeout behavior that ignores expected failure policy, misleading network errors, and webhook failures that block reconciliation or garbage collection.

live_signals: API server unable to reach webhook
reconciliation or garbage collection blocked by webhook failures

controls.name: Admission webhook availability baseline

pattern_refs.name: Webhook not reachable from API server
Admission webhook timeout ignores failurePolicy setting
Admission webhook timeout fails despite failurePolicy Ignore
Admission webhooks affected by dead TCP connections
Admission webhook global timeout exceeded with failurePolicy Ignore
Admission webhook returns misleading 400 error on network failures
Webhook admission errors block reconciliation indefinitely
Garbage collection blocked by admission webhook

guidance: Treat admission webhook reachability and timeout posture as control-plane reliability concerns, not only policy concerns.
Validate failurePolicy expectations with real network-failure tests before relying on them in production.
Watch for webhook-induced reconciliation and garbage-collection blockage after upgrades or certificate changes.

controls.summary: Detect webhook posture that turns admission into a reliability hazard or blocks reconciliation indefinitely.

controls.description: This control groups the highest-signal cluster policy migration failures we already detect natively: PodSecurityPolicy still present past its safe lifecycle, ValidatingAdmissionPolicy targets that can destabilize the control plane, and Gatekeeper-generated policy bindings that continue enforcing after the source constraint has been removed.

intent_signals: constraint and policy binding ownership metadata
live_signals: kube-controller-manager instability from policy definitions
orphaned policy bindings continuing to enforce
cluster policy state diverging from intended migration target

controls.name: Policy-control migration baseline

pattern_refs.name: Kubernetes ValidatingAdmissionPolicy crashes controller-manager with additionalProperties
PodSecurityPolicy deprecated in 1.21 removed in 1.25
Gatekeeper VAPB not removed on constraint deletion

guidance: Remove PodSecurityPolicy dependencies before Kubernetes version upgrades force the migration for you.
Validate policy-control rollout paths with real cluster state so deprecated, orphaned, or crashing policy resources do not silently become the enforcement path.
Clean up stale ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding resources when constraints are removed, and verify policy ownership boundaries after upgrades.

controls.summary: Detect policy-control posture that is deprecated, partially migrated, or still enforcing stale bindings.

controls.description: This control groups the core Argo CD Application health and sync failures that operators actually triage during broken rollouts. It is meant to support both live-state detection and higher-level intent-vs-live reasoning.

live_signals: status.sync.status
status.health.status
status.operationState.phase

example_refs.purpose: Healthy single-application Argo baseline for intent-vs-live comparison and operator explainers.

controls.name: Argo CD application reconciliation health

pattern_refs.name: Application sync failed
Application out of sync
Application health degraded
Traefik LoadBalancer Service stuck progressing in ArgoCD
ArgoCD Application stuck syncing with managed namespace PreSync

guidance: Inspect sync status, health status, and operation state together before applying a fix.
Correlate controller logs with resource-level errors so we distinguish bad intent from broken rollout execution.
Prefer intent-vs-live comparison when a repo scan is also available.

controls.summary: Detect Application states that indicate failed sync, drift, or degraded reconciliation health.

controls.description: This control covers the high-signal Flux failure modes that show up as broken GitRepository sources, failed Kustomization builds, and stalled HelmRelease reconciliation. It includes critical source readiness failures that block the entire GitOps pipeline, especially where static config and live-state evidence need to be read together.

live_signals: status.conditions
Ready False or Unknown reconciliation state
source-controller error messages

example_refs.purpose: Healthy source and kustomization baseline used to contrast stalled or cyclic reconciliation cases.
Healthy HelmRelease baseline used to explain timeout and Ready-state failures without secret or chart drift noise.

controls.name: Flux reconciliation reliability

pattern_refs.name: GitRepository not ready
Kustomization build failed
HelmRelease chart not ready
Helm release failed
Helm release pending upgrade
Flux HelmRelease stuck in Ready Unknown indefinitely

guidance: Check GitRepository authentication and network access when source is not ready.
Verify Kustomization overlay paths and patch targets when builds fail.
Confirm HelmRepository and chart references when HelmRelease cannot fetch chart.
Fix dependency cycles and timeout configuration in intent before retrying reconciliation.
Correlate live Ready or progressing state with the desired config to separate rollout lag from bad configuration.
Use live cluster evidence when investigating stuck or repeatedly failing releases.

controls.summary: Detect source, build, release, and dependency conditions that cause Flux reconciliation to stall or fail.

controls.description: This control focuses on the high-value Argo CD configuration choices that determine whether drift is corrected or silently allowed to accumulate. It is intended to answer "what does good look like?" for Application sync policy, prune behavior, and self-heal posture before teams debug broken syncs in production.

live_signals: status.sync.status
orphaned or undeleted resources after Git removal

example_refs.purpose: Recommended Argo Application sync-policy baseline with explicit prune, self-heal, and bounded retry.
Owned negative contrast example where automated sync is enabled but prune is not true.
Owned negative contrast example where automated sync is enabled but selfHeal is disabled.
Paired live snapshot showing Argo-owned resources that remain after an Application disappears from Git intent.

controls.name: Argo CD sync policy baseline

pattern_refs.name: Argo CD Application with automated sync but no prune
ArgoCD 3.x not deleting resources when app removed from Git
ArgoCD Application has auto-sync and self-heal disabled

guidance: Prefer automated sync with explicit prune posture for applications expected to converge continuously.
Enable self-heal deliberately when the platform expects Argo CD to correct manual drift.
Verify prune expectations against live orphan behavior before changing sync policy defaults on shared applications.

controls.summary: Promote the Argo CD sync-policy defaults that keep desired state and live state converged.

controls.description: This control covers the ApplicationSet-specific failure modes that operators often discover only after many applications are affected at once. It is meant to promote safer generator composition, retry behavior, and templating discipline for multi-app rollouts.

corroborating_sources: rendered generator preview or dry-run output
live_signals: controller errors creating or updating generated Applications
generated Applications diverging from intended template output

example_refs.purpose: Focused list-generator baseline with bounded rollout scope and explicit sync posture.
Owned negative contrast example where a list generator is combined with helm.valuesObject in the template source.
Owned negative contrast example where a GitLab SCM provider generator uses filters.pathsExist.
Owned negative contrast example where RollingSync template retry.limit is set to zero but generated applications already retried.
Owned negative contrast example showing a mega-ApplicationSet with too many mixed generators.

controls.name: Argo CD ApplicationSet generator safety

pattern_refs.name: Argo CD ApplicationSet with incorrect template merge behavior
ApplicationSet controller CrashLoopBackOff with helm.valuesObject and list generator
ApplicationSet GitLab generator fails with pathsExist filter
ApplicationSet progressive sync ignores retry limit configuration
ArgoCD ApplicationSet uses complex mixed generators

guidance: Prefer simpler generator combinations when broad rollout scope or template merge behavior is hard to reason about.
Validate generator output and retry semantics before relying on progressive sync or multi-source templates.
Treat ApplicationSet template changes as high-blast-radius changes and test them against representative app sets first.

controls.summary: Catch ApplicationSet generator and templating choices that create broad or surprising rollout behavior.

controls.description: This control groups the static and live-state signals that make Flux reconciliations reliable in practice: timeouts, dependency graphs, source freshness, interval configuration, and prune behavior. It is the "good Flux config" baseline that should inform both repo review and live-cluster triage.

live_signals: status.conditions
Ready Unknown or stalled reconciliation
stale source revision or unexpected retained resources

example_refs.purpose: Baseline GitRepository and Kustomization pair with explicit source freshness, timeout, retry, wait, and prune posture.
Baseline HelmRelease with explicit timing, remediation retries, and values wiring.
Owned negative contrast example showing broken source authentication and a Kustomization build failure.
Owned negative contrast example showing a Kustomization pointed at a suspended GitRepository.
Owned negative contrast example showing two HelmRelease resources in a circular dependsOn chain.
Owned negative contrast example where HelmRelease is missing spec.timeout.
Owned negative contrast example where HelmRelease disables continuous reconciliation with spec.interval: 0s.
Owned negative contrast example where HelmRelease timeout is shorter than one minute.
Paired live snapshot showing Flux-owned resources that remain after a Kustomization disappears from Git intent.

controls.name: Flux intent and reconcile baseline

pattern_refs.name: Flux HelmRelease missing timeout causing indefinite hangs
Flux prune fails when entire directory deleted from Git
HelmRelease interval zero disables continuous reconciliation
Kustomization sourceRef to suspended source uses stale revision
HelmRelease circular dependsOn not identified as root cause
HelmRelease timeout too short for reliable deployments
Kustomization not pruning deleted resources from Git

guidance: Set timeout and interval values deliberately so Flux continues reconciling and fails fast enough to debug.
Keep dependency graphs acyclic and verify source references do not point at suspended or stale inputs.
Treat prune-related changes as potentially destructive and confirm expected live deletions before rollout.

controls.summary: Define the key Flux config defaults that prevent reconcile stalls, stale source usage, and drift after Git changes.

controls.description: This control surfaces the high-signal drift conditions that indicate live cluster state has diverged from Git-declared intent. It covers both manual kubectl edits and image tag drift that break the GitOps promise of source-of-truth enforcement. These patterns are essential for the intent-vs-live comparison story and apply to both ArgoCD and Flux managed workloads.

live_signals: metadata.annotations['kubectl.kubernetes.io/last-applied-configuration']
actual running container images
live resource spec differing from Git source

example_refs.purpose: Git intent example that can be contrasted with live state to show drift.
Live cluster snapshot showing drifted resources with different image tags.

controls.name: GitOps drift detection

pattern_refs.name: Manual kubectl edit detected
Image tag drift

guidance: Identify the source of drift (manual edit, auto-updater, CI, or emergency patch).
Determine if drift should be permanent (update Git to match live) or reverted (sync to restore intent).
Enable drift alerting to detect future manual changes before they cause sync failures.
Use image digests or specific version tags to prevent unintended image drift.

controls.summary: Detect configuration drift between Git intent and live cluster state across ArgoCD and Flux environments.

controls.description: This control groups the deterministic NetworkPolicy failures that operators repeatedly trip over in real clusters: controllers blocked by policy, readiness traffic denied, and policy objects that no longer select any pods. It is intended as a baseline for "tighten traffic without breaking the platform."

live_signals: selected pods missing or unexpected traffic failures
readiness probe failures caused by policy

controls.name: NetworkPolicy connectivity baseline

pattern_refs.name: Dangling NetworkPolicy (no matching pods)
NetworkPolicy blocks ALB ingress traffic
NetworkPolicy blocks nginx-ingress-controller
Egress NetworkPolicy blocks readiness probe
Egress NetworkPolicy blocks kubelet readiness probe

guidance: Start with default-deny intent, then explicitly allow ingress-controller and readiness paths that the platform needs.
Remove or repair NetworkPolicy objects that no longer match real workloads.
Validate policy changes against live traffic paths before rollout, especially for ingress and health probes.

controls.summary: Detect NetworkPolicy posture that unintentionally breaks ingress, readiness, or leaves stale policy objects behind.

controls.description: This control packages the high-signal Ingress configuration checks that determine whether traffic actually lands on the intended controller with the intended TLS posture. It is designed to answer the operator question "is this ingress configured in a sane, routable, HTTPS-first way?"

live_signals: controller ignoring or rejecting Ingress objects
fake or temporary certificates served

controls.name: Ingress class and TLS baseline

pattern_refs.name: TLS certificate secret not found
NGINX Ingress serving fake certificate - TLS secret not found
Ingress cannot disable HTTP access
Ingress TLS secret cannot reference other namespaces
IngressClass not found when Ingress specifies ingressClassName
Multiple default IngressClass conflict blocks Ingress creation
Ingress stuck without matching IngressClass - silently ignored
IngressClass controller string mismatch causes controller to ignore class

guidance: Ensure every Ingress resolves to one intended IngressClass and one working controller string.
Treat missing TLS secrets and silent class mismatches as deployment blockers, not low-priority hygiene issues.
Prefer an explicit HTTPS-first stance when exposing applications through Ingress.

controls.summary: Detect Ingress defaults that leave HTTP exposed, break class resolution, or fail TLS secret wiring.

controls.description: This control captures the annotation-driven ingress-nginx risks that move beyond simple misrouting into direct security exposure. It is meant to keep annotation flexibility from becoming an unreviewed code-injection path.

live_signals: controller accepting risky annotations
unexpected config generation or route behavior

controls.name: Ingress annotation safety

pattern_refs.name: Ingress nginx configuration-snippet annotation injection vulnerability
Ingress nginx permanent-redirect annotation code injection
Ingress-nginx annotation validation bypass
Ingress-nginx annotation validation bypass vulnerability (CVE-2024-7646)
Ingress nginx annotation injection vulnerability
Ingress nginx permanent-redirect annotation injection

guidance: Restrict dangerous ingress-nginx annotations and review any snippet-like configuration as code execution risk.
Treat annotation validation bypasses as controller-security issues, not just routing bugs.
Prefer safer controller features or curated config surfaces over arbitrary annotation snippets.

controls.summary: Detect dangerous ingress-nginx annotation usage and validation bypass conditions that turn Ingress into an injection surface.

controls.description: This control promotes the high-signal Gateway API failures that operators repeatedly hit when moving beyond classic Ingress: routes attached to the wrong parent, missing timeout posture, unsupported route semantics, and cross-namespace or TLS reference wiring that looks valid in YAML but fails in practice. It is the first promoted Gateway/public-exposure baseline in this repo family.

live_signals: routes attached to missing or incompatible parents
ReferenceGrant or Secret access failures
route or listener status diverging from expected Gateway behavior

controls.name: Gateway API route and TLS baseline

pattern_refs.name: Gateway API HTTPRoute parentRef not matching Gateway
BackendTLSPolicy conflicts with TLS passthrough
TLSRoute SNI match inconsistent with HTTPRoute hostnames
GRPCRoute lacks BackendTLSPolicy support
GRPCRoute missing request timeout for streaming RPC
TCPRoute and UDPRoute lack hostname support
Gateway secret reference requires ReferenceGrant for cross-namespace

guidance: Treat parentRef, ReferenceGrant, and route-kind compatibility as deployment blockers for Gateway API changes.
Configure explicit timeout and TLS posture for Gateway API routes instead of relying on implementation-specific defaults.
Validate cross-namespace Secret and backend references with live status, not only static manifest review.

controls.summary: Detect Gateway API attachment, timeout, cross-namespace reference, and TLS semantics that leave routes present but not actually safe or routable.

controls.description: This control groups the RBAC permissions and bindings that most often create immediate privilege-escalation or credential-extraction paths: nodes/proxy, pods/exec, secrets read, service account token minting, CSR approval, and wildcard resource access. It is intended as the operator-facing baseline for "do not grant this broadly" RBAC posture.

live_signals: bound subjects with high-risk permissions in active cluster RBAC objects

controls.name: RBAC privilege boundary baseline

pattern_refs.name: ClusterRole grants nodes/proxy enabling Kubelet API RCE
ClusterRoleBinding binds nodes/proxy ClusterRole to subjects
ClusterRole grants pods/exec create
ClusterRole grants secrets read permissions
ClusterRole grants serviceaccounts/token create
ClusterRole grants CSR approval update permissions
ClusterRole uses wildcard resources with broad verbs

guidance: Remove or narrow broad ClusterRole permissions before adding compensating controls.
Treat nodes/proxy, pods/exec, secrets read, serviceaccounts/token create, and wildcard resource access as exceptional permissions requiring explicit review.
Review every subject bound to high-risk ClusterRoles, not just the role content itself.

controls.summary: Detect the highest-risk RBAC permissions that turn broad access into direct cluster compromise paths.

controls.description: This control focuses on the identity-side RBAC mistakes that are easy to miss in reviews: broken RoleBinding scope assumptions and unnecessary automounted service account credentials on workloads that do not need them. It is meant to make service-account identity exposure visible before it turns into lateral movement or confusing auth failures.

corroborating_sources: mounted token or projected volume inspection
live_signals: effective RoleBinding scope in cluster
running workloads with unnecessary service account tokens mounted

controls.name: Service account identity baseline

pattern_refs.name: RoleBinding to non-existent ClusterRole enables privilege escalation
RoleBinding to non-existent ClusterRole creates security risk
RoleBinding with ClusterRole does not restrict to namespace
Deployment does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
StatefulSet does not disable automountServiceAccountToken
DaemonSet does not disable automountServiceAccountToken

guidance: Make RoleBinding scope explicit and avoid bindings whose real privilege boundary is ambiguous or missing.
Disable automountServiceAccountToken by default on workloads that do not need Kubernetes API credentials.
Review service account intent together with bound roles so identity exposure and permission scope are evaluated together.

controls.summary: Detect RoleBinding and workload identity defaults that quietly widen effective service account access.

controls.description: This control groups the high-signal external secret management failures that operators actually need surfaced together: auth validation problems, deleted SecretStore references, broken refresh semantics, TLS verification failures, and secrets that never get recreated after external deletion.

live_signals: status.conditions
target Secret missing or stale after external change

controls.name: External secret store baseline

pattern_refs.name: External Secrets store authentication validation failure
External Secrets silent sync failure - Secret not created
ExternalSecret refresh interval too aggressive causing rate limits
ExternalSecret referencing deleted SecretStore
External Secrets sync fails silently on provider error
External Secrets refreshInterval zero disables refresh
external-secrets sync fails silently
external-secrets refreshInterval zero disables sync
TLS certificate verification failure for SecretStore connections
Secrets not regenerated when deleted externally

guidance: Validate SecretStore authentication and TLS trust before debugging downstream missing secrets.
Use non-zero refresh intervals and verify external deletion or rotation paths are exercised safely.
Treat silent sync failures as platform incidents, not low-priority drift.

controls.summary: Detect ExternalSecret and SecretStore posture that quietly breaks sync, trust, or secret regeneration.

controls.description: This control focuses on the operator-facing secret hygiene failures that make credentials easy to leak: secrets stored in ConfigMaps, overly permissive credential files, plaintext logging, and cleartext password material embedded in generated configs.

intent_signals: logging or rendered config values carrying credential material
live_signals: mounted credential files readable too broadly
cleartext credentials visible in generated config or logs

controls.name: Secret handling and exposure baseline

pattern_refs.name: Kubernetes ConfigMap/Secret mount permissions world-readable by default
Loki S3 credentials exposed in ConfigMap instead of Secret
Credential files have overly permissive access
Azure credentials logged in plaintext with JSON format
Vector Helm chart leaks AWS credentials in ConfigMap
MetalLB BGP password stored in clear text in FRR configuration

guidance: Keep credential material in Secrets or external stores, not ConfigMaps or generated plaintext configs.
Lock down credential file permissions and remove plaintext credential logging paths.
Treat cleartext passwords and credential leaks as security issues even when the workload still functions.

controls.summary: Detect credential handling patterns that expose secrets through ConfigMaps, permissive file access, or plaintext logs.

controls.description: This control packages the first high-signal workload hardening checks that should be visible to operators as one baseline: privilege, host namespace access, root execution, privilege escalation, immutable filesystem posture, and capability dropping.
controls.name: Workload security context baseline

pattern_refs.name: Deployment container runs in privileged mode
Deployment uses host namespaces
Container does not set runAsNonRoot
Container does not disable allowPrivilegeEscalation
Container does not enforce readOnlyRootFilesystem
Container does not drop ALL capabilities

guidance: Remove privileged and host-namespace access unless there is an explicit, reviewed platform need.
Set non-root execution, disable allowPrivilegeEscalation, enforce readOnlyRootFilesystem, and drop ALL capabilities by default.
Treat exceptions as narrow waivers, not baseline config.

controls.summary: Detect dangerous workload security-context defaults that materially weaken container isolation.

controls.description: This control groups the minimum resource-governance baseline that should be visible in operator scorecards and CI gates. It starts with resource limits because that signal is deterministic and widely actionable.
controls.name: Workload resource governance baseline

pattern_refs.name: Deployment containers omit resource limits

guidance: Set CPU and memory limits for every container and pair them with requests.
Consider namespace-level defaults where teams repeatedly omit limits.
Treat missing limits as a reliability problem, not only a policy problem.

controls.summary: Detect missing resource limits that lead to noisy-neighbor behavior and unstable scheduling.

frameworks.description: This framework groups the first promoted controls that describe secure and resilient cluster defaults. It is aimed at platform teams who need a stable view over cluster-hardening posture rather than isolated upgrade bugs.
frameworks.name: Cluster hardening
outcomes: Surface the first stable cluster-hardening controls in one operator-facing bundle.
Keep pod security boundaries, control-plane certificates, admission reliability, and policy-control migration visible together.
Provide a clean starting point for future API server, scheduler, and kubelet hardening controls.
frameworks.summary: Curated controls for pod security boundaries, control-plane certificates, and admission reliability.

frameworks.description: This framework groups the first operator-facing controls for Argo CD and Flux. It is aimed at teams who need to distinguish desired-state defects from rollout and reconciliation failures.
frameworks.name: GitOps operators
outcomes: Surface the highest-signal GitOps health failures in one operator-facing bundle.
Encourage intent-vs-live comparison instead of treating every failure as the same class of problem.
Make healthy Argo CD and Flux configuration defaults visible as promoted controls, not just scattered pattern IDs.
Keep multi-application generator and reconcile-path safety in the same framework as live health failures.
Provide a stable framework surface before the full pattern corpus is promoted into controls.
frameworks.summary: Curated controls for diagnosing intent, reconciliation, and live-state problems in GitOps-managed workloads.

controls.name: Gateway API route and TLS baseline

frameworks.description: This framework groups the first promoted controls for network-facing Kubernetes configuration. It is intended to help teams reason about safe exposure, controller selection, TLS posture, Gateway API semantics, and policy-driven connectivity in one place.
frameworks.name: Network exposure
outcomes: Make safe service exposure, Gateway API route semantics, and policy-driven connectivity visible as one stable framework view.
Separate ingress, Gateway API, and NetworkPolicy baseline posture from the broader exploratory pattern corpus.
Provide a clean operator-facing bundle for classic Ingress and newer Gateway API exposure paths.
frameworks.summary: Curated controls for ingress, TLS, and NetworkPolicy posture that keep services reachable without overexposure.

frameworks.description: This framework curates the highest-value promoted controls across GitOps, workload hardening, identity, exposure, secrets, and cluster safety. It is meant to answer "what good looks like" for platform teams who want a focused operational baseline instead of a broad compliance checklist.
frameworks.name: Platform best
outcomes: Give platform teams one baseline view that crosses family boundaries instead of forcing them to assemble controls manually.
Keep GitOps health, workload safety, identity posture, network exposure, secret hygiene, and admission safeguards in one operator-facing bundle.
Provide a practical "good configuration" starter surface for future explanations, scorecards, and AI-assisted triage.
frameworks.summary: Opinionated cross-family baseline for running GitOps-managed Kubernetes platforms with safer defaults.

frameworks.description: This framework groups the first promoted identity and authorization controls for Kubernetes operators. It is aimed at teams who need a stable baseline for privilege boundaries and service-account exposure, not just one-off RBAC findings.
frameworks.name: RBAC and identity
outcomes: Surface the highest-risk RBAC permissions and bindings in one operator-facing bundle.
Make service-account token exposure part of identity posture, not an afterthought.
Provide a stable framework view for future identity and auth-related promoted controls.
frameworks.summary: Curated controls for dangerous Kubernetes permissions, binding scope mistakes, and service-account identity exposure.

frameworks.description: This framework groups the first promoted secret-management controls for Kubernetes operators. It is intended to keep secret sync, storage, and exposure problems visible as one stable workflow surface instead of scattered product-specific findings.
frameworks.name: Secrets and credentials
outcomes: Surface external secret sync and trust failures in one operator-facing bundle.
Make credential exposure and unsafe secret handling visible as first-class controls.
Provide a stable framework surface for future secret rotation and identity-integrated controls.
frameworks.summary: Curated controls for secret lifecycle, external secret sync, and credential exposure posture.

frameworks.description: This framework packages the first promoted workload hardening controls for container isolation and resource governance. It is intended as a baseline workflow surface for CI and operator scorecards.
frameworks.name: Workload hardening
outcomes: Make the first secure-by-default workload checks visible as one stable framework view.
Separate operator-facing baseline checks from the broader exploratory pattern corpus.
Provide a clean starting point for future framework bundles like network exposure and identity hardening.
frameworks.summary: Curated controls for secure-by-default workload configuration and resource governance.
